input.description:
FieldDef.id is bare `ulid::Ulid`. FieldsContext uses `HashMap<Ulid, usize>` for id_index. All methods that accept or return field IDs use bare Ulid. Need a FieldDefId newtype using define_id! macro.

~30 bare Ulid usages across types.rs, context.rs, compute.rs, validation.rs plus downstream in kanban defaults.rs.

- [ ] Add `FieldDefId` to swissarmyhammer-fields/src/id_types.rs via define_id!
- [ ] Change FieldDef.id from Ulid to FieldDefId
- [ ] Update FieldsContext id_index and all methods
- [ ] Update all test code constructing FieldDef
- [ ] Update kanban defaults.rs
- [ ] Re-export from lib.rs
- [ ] Run full test suite
input.title: Complete FieldDefId newtype — eliminate bare Ulid in fields crate

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

React context-based command scope system. Commands live in scopes that nest with the component tree. Resolution walks up from the focused element's nearest scope.

## What to build

### CommandScope React context provider
- `<CommandScope commands={[...]}>` wraps a subtree
- Each scope registers commands (id, name, description, keys, execute, available)
- Scopes nest — child providers shadow parent providers

### Scope chain resolution
- Given a command id, walk up from current scope to root
- Deepest matching command wins (shadowing)
- `available: false` blocks upward walk (blocking)
- Unregistered commands pass through to parent

### useAvailableCommands() hook
- Returns all commands visible from the current scope
- Grouped by scope depth (global, view, grid, etc.)
- Used by the command palette and :help

### Command interface
```typescript
interface Command {
  id: string
  name: string
  description?: string
  keys?: { vim?: string; cua?: string; emacs?: string }
  execute: () => Promise<void>  // or sync
  available?: boolean  // default true
}
```

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/command-scope.tsx` — CommandScope provider, useCommandScope, useAvailableCommands
- Tests for scope resolution, shadowing, blocking, pass-through

## Checklist
- [ ] CommandScope provider component
- [ ] Scope chain resolution logic
- [ ] Shadowing (deeper scope same command id wins)
- [ ] Blocking (available: false stops upward walk)
- [ ] Pass-through (unregistered commands walk to parent)
- [ ] useAvailableCommands() hook
- [ ] useExecuteCommand(id) hook
- [ ] Tests for all resolution behaviors
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: CommandScope provider and scope chain resolution

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Keybindings map key sequences to command IDs. The binding table is global per keymap mode. Resolution of the command ID goes through the scope chain.

## What to build

### Binding table
- One table per keymap mode (vim, cua, emacs)
- Maps key sequence string → command ID
- Mod = Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on Windows/Linux
- Multi-key sequences (gg, dd, zo) with ~500ms timeout pending-key buffer

### Key handler
- Global keydown listener
- Looks up key sequence in binding table for current keymap mode
- Gets command ID
- Resolves command ID through scope chain (from CommandScope)
- If resolved and available → execute
- If not resolved → unhandled (let browser handle it)

### Pending key buffer for vim
- When first key of a multi-key sequence is pressed, start buffer
- ~500ms timeout — if second key doesn't arrive, flush as single key
- Lookup table of 1-2 key sequences, not a full vim parser

### Initial bindings (from architecture doc)
```
vim:
  ":": app.command
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  u: app.undo
  Mod+R: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss

cua:
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  Mod+Z: app.undo
  Mod+Shift+Z: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss

emacs:
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  C-/: app.undo
  C-?: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss
```

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/keybindings.ts` — binding tables, key handler, pending buffer
- `ui/src/lib/keybindings.test.ts` — tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Binding table data structure per keymap mode
- [ ] Key handler (global keydown → lookup → scope resolve → execute)
- [ ] Mod key resolution (Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on others)
- [ ] Multi-key pending buffer with timeout for vim
- [ ] Integration with CommandScope (resolve command ID through scope chain)
- [ ] Don't capture keys when CM6 editor has focus (CM6 handles its own keys)
- [ ] Initial vim/cua/emacs binding tables from architecture doc
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: Keybinding layer — key sequences to command IDs with scope resolution

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Single command palette popover for both `:` and `Mod+Shift+P`. Uses CM6 single-line as the filter input (not a plain input — vim motions work inside).

## What to build

### Palette component
- Plain portal + backdrop (not Radix Popover — avoids focus trap conflicts with CM6)
- Centered on viewport
- CM6 single-line input for filter text, with user's keymap mode
- Fuzzy search filters the command list as you type
- Shows keybinding hints for current keymap mode
- Grouped by scope depth (global, view, grid)
- Escape dismisses
- Enter on selected command executes it
- Arrow keys / j/k navigate the list

### CM6 integration
- Single-line CM6 instance with the user's selected keymap (vim/cua/emacs)
- Escape handling: in vim mode, first Escape goes insert→normal, second Escape dismisses palette
- onChange fires fuzzy filter

### Command data
- Uses useAvailableCommands() from CommandScope
- Each command shows: name, keybinding hint for current mode
- Fuzzy match on command name and description

### Two entry points (same UI)
- `:` opens palette (app.command) — CM6 input pre-focused
- `Mod+Shift+P` opens palette (app.palette) — same UI, same list

## Files
- `ui/src/components/command-palette.tsx` — the palette component
- `ui/src/lib/fuzzy-filter.ts` — fuzzy matching utility
- Tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Portal-rendered centered popover with backdrop
- [ ] CM6 single-line input with keymap mode
- [ ] Fuzzy search filtering
- [ ] Command list grouped by scope depth
- [ ] Keybinding hints per command
- [ ] Arrow key / j/k navigation
- [ ] Enter executes, Escape dismisses
- [ ] Vim Escape: insert→normal first, then dismiss
- [ ] Wire up app.command and app.palette commands
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: Command palette UI — portal + CM6 input + fuzzy search

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

The client-side undo stack holds Command objects — not just IDs. Each command has do/undo/redo methods. Some commands are pure client-side (view switch), some dispatch to the Tauri backend and hold the operation ULID for backend undo/redo.

## What to build

### Command interface
```typescript
interface Command {
  do(): Promise<void>     // execute, may call backend, captures result state
  undo(): Promise<void>   // reverse — may call backend undo(ulid) or flip UI state
  redo(): Promise<void>   // re-apply — may call backend redo(ulid) or flip UI state
  label: string           // for display: "Set Status → Done"
}
```

### Undo stack
- Bounded array (~100 entries), old entries fall off bottom
- Pointer separates undo side from redo side
- `do()` pushes command, clears redo side
- `undo()` pops from undo side, moves to redo side
- `redo()` pops from redo side, moves to undo side

### Backend-bound commands
```typescript
class SetFieldCommand implements Command {
  private operationId?: string  // filled after do()
  async do() { result = await invoke("update_entity_field", ...); this.operationId = result.operation_id }
  async undo() { await invoke("undo_operation", { id: this.operationId }) }
  async redo() { await invoke("redo_operation", { id: this.operationId }) }
}
```

### Client-only commands (future — view switching etc)
```typescript
class SwitchViewCommand implements Command {
  async do() { this.setActiveView(this.to) }
  async undo() { this.setActiveView(this.from) }
  async redo() { this.setActiveView(this.to) }
}
```

### React integration
- `useUndoStack()` hook — exposes undo(), redo(), canUndo, canRedo
- app.undo and app.redo global commands dispatch through this hook

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/undo-stack.ts` — UndoStack class, Command interface
- `ui/src/lib/commands/set-field-command.ts` — backend-bound command
- `ui/src/lib/undo-context.tsx` — React context + useUndoStack hook
- Tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Command interface (do/undo/redo/label)
- [ ] UndoStack class (bounded, pointer-based)
- [ ] SetFieldCommand (backend-bound, stores operation_id)
- [ ] useUndoStack() React hook
- [ ] Wire app.undo and app.redo global commands to the stack
- [ ] Tests for stack operations (push, undo, redo, clear redo on new do, bounded size)
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: Client-side undo stack with Command objects (do/undo/redo)

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Bottom bar showing the current mode (Normal, Command, Search) plus context info.

## What to build

### Bottom bar component
- Fixed at bottom of the app
- Shows mode: `-- NORMAL --`, `-- COMMAND --`, `-- SEARCH --`
- Shows active view name (for Phase 2, placeholder for now)
- Shows sort/filter indicator (for Phase 5, placeholder for now)

### Mode state
- Three modes: Normal, Command, Search
- Normal: keystrokes dispatch commands via binding table → scope chain
- Command: palette is open (launched by : or Mod+Shift+P)
- Search: / (vim) or Mod+F (cua) — future, placeholder for now

### React integration
- `useAppMode()` hook — exposes current mode and setter
- Mode context provider wraps the app
- Command palette opening sets mode to Command, closing sets back to Normal

## Files
- `ui/src/components/mode-indicator.tsx` — the bottom bar
- `ui/src/lib/app-mode-context.tsx` — mode state context
- Tests

## Checklist
- [ ] AppMode context provider (Normal/Command/Search)
- [ ] useAppMode() hook
- [ ] ModeIndicator bottom bar component
- [ ] Mode switches when palette opens/closes
- [ ] Style matches architecture doc layout
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: Mode indicator bottom bar — Normal/Command/Search

input.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Wire everything together: wrap the app in the global CommandScope, register global commands, integrate keybindings, palette, undo stack, and mode indicator into the existing App.tsx.

## Global commands to register (from architecture doc)
- `app.command` — opens command palette (`:`)
- `app.palette` — opens command palette (`Mod+Shift+P`)
- `app.undo` — pops undo stack, dispatches undo
- `app.redo` — pops redo stack, dispatches redo
- `app.dismiss` — closes palette/panels (Escape)
- `app.save` — placeholder for now
- `app.search` — placeholder for now
- `app.keymap` — switch keymap mode
- `app.theme` — placeholder for now
- `app.help` — placeholder for now

## What to build

### AppShell wrapper
- Wraps the existing App component tree
- Provides: CommandScope (global), KeybindingProvider, UndoStackProvider, AppModeProvider
- Registers global commands in the root CommandScope

### Integration
- Replace the ad-hoc Escape handler in App.tsx with the command system (app.dismiss)
- Remove hardcoded keydown listeners — everything goes through keybindings → scope chain
- Command palette renders at the app level (portal)
- Mode indicator renders at the bottom

### Phase 1 scope chain should look like:
```
CommandScope (global)
  └─ <AppShell />     ← focused

:help works. Mod+Shift+P shows global commands. : opens same palette.
```

## Files
- `ui/src/components/app-shell.tsx` — wraps App with all providers
- Modify `ui/src/App.tsx` — remove ad-hoc key handlers, add palette + mode bar
- Modify `ui/src/main.tsx` — wrap with AppShell if needed

## Checklist
- [ ] Create AppShell component with all providers
- [ ] Register all global commands
- [ ] Wire app.command and app.palette to open the palette
- [ ] Wire app.undo and app.redo to the undo stack
- [ ] Wire app.dismiss to close palette and panels
- [ ] Remove ad-hoc Escape handler from App.tsx
- [ ] Render CommandPalette at app level
- [ ] Render ModeIndicator at bottom
- [ ] Verify : opens palette in vim mode
- [ ] Verify Mod+Shift+P opens palette in all modes
- [ ] Verify u undoes in vim mode, Mod+Z in cua mode
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
input.title: Register global commands and wire up AppShell with CommandScope

output.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

React context-based command scope system. Commands live in scopes that nest with the component tree. Resolution walks up from the focused element's nearest scope.

## What to build

### CommandScope React context provider
- `<CommandScope commands={[...]}>` wraps a subtree
- Each scope registers commands (id, name, description, keys, execute, available)
- Scopes nest — child providers shadow parent providers

### Scope chain resolution
- Given a command id, walk up from current scope to root
- Deepest matching command wins (shadowing)
- `available: false` blocks upward walk (blocking)
- Unregistered commands pass through to parent

### useAvailableCommands() hook
- Returns all commands visible from the current scope
- Grouped by scope depth (global, view, grid, etc.)
- Used by the command palette and :help

### Command interface
```typescript
interface Command {
  id: string
  name: string
  description?: string
  keys?: { vim?: string; cua?: string; emacs?: string }
  execute: () => Promise<void>  // or sync
  available?: boolean  // default true
}
```

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/command-scope.tsx` — CommandScope provider, useCommandScope, useAvailableCommands
- Tests for scope resolution, shadowing, blocking, pass-through

## Checklist
- [ ] CommandScope provider component
- [ ] Scope chain resolution logic
- [ ] Shadowing (deeper scope same command id wins)
- [ ] Blocking (available: false stops upward walk)
- [ ] Pass-through (unregistered commands walk to parent)
- [ ] useAvailableCommands() hook
- [ ] useExecuteCommand(id) hook
- [ ] Tests for all resolution behaviors
- [ ] Run test suite
output.title: CommandScope provider and scope chain resolution

output.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Keybindings map key sequences to command IDs. The binding table is global per keymap mode. Resolution of the command ID goes through the scope chain.

## What to build

### Binding table
- One table per keymap mode (vim, cua, emacs)
- Maps key sequence string → command ID
- Mod = Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on Windows/Linux
- Multi-key sequences (gg, dd, zo) with ~500ms timeout pending-key buffer

### Key handler
- Global keydown listener
- Looks up key sequence in binding table for current keymap mode
- Gets command ID
- Resolves command ID through scope chain (from CommandScope)
- If resolved and available → execute
- If not resolved → unhandled (let browser handle it)

### Pending key buffer for vim
- When first key of a multi-key sequence is pressed, start buffer
- ~500ms timeout — if second key doesn't arrive, flush as single key
- Lookup table of 1-2 key sequences, not a full vim parser

### Initial bindings (from architecture doc)
```
vim:
  ":": app.command
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  u: app.undo
  Mod+R: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss

cua:
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  Mod+Z: app.undo
  Mod+Shift+Z: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss

emacs:
  Mod+Shift+P: app.palette
  C-/: app.undo
  C-?: app.redo
  Escape: app.dismiss
```

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/keybindings.ts` — binding tables, key handler, pending buffer
- `ui/src/lib/keybindings.test.ts` — tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Binding table data structure per keymap mode
- [ ] Key handler (global keydown → lookup → scope resolve → execute)
- [ ] Mod key resolution (Cmd on Mac, Ctrl on others)
- [ ] Multi-key pending buffer with timeout for vim
- [ ] Integration with CommandScope (resolve command ID through scope chain)
- [ ] Don't capture keys when CM6 editor has focus (CM6 handles its own keys)
- [ ] Initial vim/cua/emacs binding tables from architecture doc
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
output.title: Keybinding layer — key sequences to command IDs with scope resolution

output.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

The client-side undo stack holds Command objects — not just IDs. Each command has do/undo/redo methods. Some commands are pure client-side (view switch), some dispatch to the Tauri backend and hold the operation ULID for backend undo/redo.

## What to build

### Command interface
```typescript
interface Command {
  do(): Promise<void>     // execute, may call backend, captures result state
  undo(): Promise<void>   // reverse — may call backend undo(ulid) or flip UI state
  redo(): Promise<void>   // re-apply — may call backend redo(ulid) or flip UI state
  label: string           // for display: "Set Status → Done"
}
```

### Undo stack
- Bounded array (~100 entries), old entries fall off bottom
- Pointer separates undo side from redo side
- `do()` pushes command, clears redo side
- `undo()` pops from undo side, moves to redo side
- `redo()` pops from redo side, moves to undo side

### Backend-bound commands
```typescript
class SetFieldCommand implements Command {
  private operationId?: string  // filled after do()
  async do() { result = await invoke("update_entity_field", ...); this.operationId = result.operation_id }
  async undo() { await invoke("undo_operation", { id: this.operationId }) }
  async redo() { await invoke("redo_operation", { id: this.operationId }) }
}
```

### Client-only commands (future — view switching etc)
```typescript
class SwitchViewCommand implements Command {
  async do() { this.setActiveView(this.to) }
  async undo() { this.setActiveView(this.from) }
  async redo() { this.setActiveView(this.to) }
}
```

### React integration
- `useUndoStack()` hook — exposes undo(), redo(), canUndo, canRedo
- app.undo and app.redo global commands dispatch through this hook

## Files
- `ui/src/lib/undo-stack.ts` — UndoStack class, Command interface
- `ui/src/lib/commands/set-field-command.ts` — backend-bound command
- `ui/src/lib/undo-context.tsx` — React context + useUndoStack hook
- Tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Command interface (do/undo/redo/label)
- [ ] UndoStack class (bounded, pointer-based)
- [ ] SetFieldCommand (backend-bound, stores operation_id)
- [ ] useUndoStack() React hook
- [ ] Wire app.undo and app.redo global commands to the stack
- [ ] Tests for stack operations (push, undo, redo, clear redo on new do, bounded size)
- [ ] Run test suite
output.title: Client-side undo stack with Command objects (do/undo/redo)

Single command palette popover for both `:` and `Mod+Shift+P`. Uses CM6 single-line as the filter input (not a plain input — vim motions work inside).

## What to build

### Palette component
- Plain portal + backdrop (not Radix Popover — avoids focus trap conflicts with CM6)
- Centered on viewport
- CM6 single-line input for filter text, with user's keymap mode
- Fuzzy search filters the command list as you type
- Shows keybinding hints for current keymap mode
- Grouped by scope depth (global, view, grid)
- Escape dismisses
- Enter on selected command executes it
- Arrow keys / j/k navigate the list

### CM6 integration
- Single-line CM6 instance with the user's selected keymap (vim/cua/emacs)
- Escape handling: in vim mode, first Escape goes insert→normal, second Escape dismisses palette
- onChange fires fuzzy filter

### Command data
- Uses useAvailableCommands() from CommandScope
- Each command shows: name, keybinding hint for current mode
- Fuzzy match on command name and description

### Two entry points (same UI)
- `:` opens palette (app.command) — CM6 input pre-focused
- `Mod+Shift+P` opens palette (app.palette) — same UI, same list

## Files
- `ui/src/components/command-palette.tsx` — the palette component
- `ui/src/lib/fuzzy-filter.ts` — fuzzy matching utility
- Tests

## Checklist
- [ ] Portal-rendered centered popover with backdrop
- [ ] CM6 single-line input with keymap mode
- [ ] Fuzzy search filtering
- [ ] Command list grouped by scope depth
- [ ] Keybinding hints per command
- [ ] Arrow key / j/k navigation
- [ ] Enter executes, Escape dismisses
- [ ] Vim Escape: insert→normal first, then dismiss

Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Wire everything together: wrap the app in the global CommandScope, register global commands, integrate keybindings, palette, undo stack, and mode indicator into the existing App.tsx.

## Global commands to register (from architecture doc)
- `app.command` — opens command palette (`:`)
- `app.palette` — opens command palette (`Mod+Shift+P`)
- `app.undo` — pops undo stack, dispatches undo
- `app.redo` — pops redo stack, dispatches redo
- `app.dismiss` — closes palette/panels (Escape)
- `app.save` — placeholder for now
- `app.search` — placeholder for now
- `app.keymap` — switch keymap mode
- `app.theme` — placeholder for now
- `app.help` — placeholder for now

## What to build

### AppShell wrapper
- Wraps the existing App component tree
- Provides: CommandScope (global), KeybindingProvider, UndoStackProvider, AppModeProvider
- Registers global commands in the root CommandScope

### Integration
- Replace the ad-hoc Escape handler in App.tsx with the command system (app.dismiss)
- Remove hardcoded keydown listeners — everything goes through keybindings → scope chain
- Command palette renders at the app level (portal)
- Mode indicator renders at the bottom

### Phase 1 scope chain should look like:
```
CommandScope (global)
  └─ <AppShell />     ← focused

:help works. Mod+Shift+P shows global commands. : opens same palette.
```

## Files
- `ui/src/components/app-shell.tsx` — wraps App with all providers
- Modify `ui/src/App.tsx` — remove ad-hoc key handlers, add palette + mode bar
- Modify `ui/src/main.tsx` — wrap with AppShell if needed

## Checklist
- [ ] Create AppShell component with all providers
- [ ] Register all global commands
- [ ] Wire app.command and app.palette to open the palette
- [ ] Wire app.undo and app.redo to the undo stack
- [ ] Wire app.dismiss to close palette and panels
- [ ] Remove ad-hoc Escape handler from App.tsx
- [ ] Render CommandPalette at app level
- [ ] Render ModeIndicator at bottom
- [ ] Verify : opens palette in vim mode
- [ ] Verify Mod+Shift+P opens palette in all modes
- [ ] Verify u undoes in vim mode, Mod+Z in cua mode
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
output.title: Register global commands and wire up AppShell with CommandScope

output.description:
Phase 1 deliverable from app-architecture.md.

Wire everything together: wrap the app in the global CommandScope, register global commands, integrate keybindings, palette, undo stack, and mode indicator into the existing App.tsx.

## Global commands to register (from architecture doc)
- `app.command` — opens command palette (`:`)
- `app.palette` — opens command palette (`Mod+Shift+P`)
- `app.undo` — pops undo stack, dispatches undo
- `app.redo` — pops redo stack, dispatches redo
- `app.dismiss` — closes palette/panels (Escape)
- `app.save` — placeholder for now
- `app.search` — placeholder for now
- `app.keymap` — switch keymap mode
- `app.theme` — placeholder for now
- `app.help` — placeholder for now

## What to build

### AppShell wrapper
- Wraps the existing App component tree
- Provides: CommandScope (global), KeybindingProvider, UndoStackProvider, AppModeProvider
- Registers global commands in the root CommandScope

### Integration
- Replace the ad-hoc Escape handler in App.tsx with the command system (app.dismiss)
- Remove hardcoded keydown listeners — everything goes through keybindings → scope chain
- Command palette renders at the app level (portal)
- Mode indicator renders at the bottom

### Phase 1 scope chain should look like:
```
CommandScope (global)
  └─ <AppShell />     ← focused

:help works. Mod+Shift+P shows global commands. : opens same palette.
```

## Files
- `ui/src/components/app-shell.tsx` — wraps App with all providers
- Modify `ui/src/App.tsx` — remove ad-hoc key handlers, add palette + mode bar
- Modify `ui/src/main.tsx` — wrap with AppShell if needed

## Checklist
- [ ] Create AppShell component with all providers
- [ ] Register all global commands
- [ ] Wire app.command and app.palette to open the palette
- [ ] Wire app.undo and app.redo to the undo stack
- [ ] Wire app.dismiss to close palette and panels
- [ ] Remove ad-hoc Escape handler from App.tsx
- [ ] Render CommandPalette at app level
- [ ] Render ModeIndicator at bottom
- [ ] Verify : opens palette in vim mode
- [ ] Verify Mod+Shift+P opens palette in all modes
- [ ] Verify u undoes in vim mode, Mod+Z in cua mode
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Run test suite
output.title: Register global commands and wire up AppShell with CommandScope

input.description:
The frontend has hardcoded TypeScript interfaces (Task, Tag, Column, Board) that mirror entity fields as typed properties. This is fundamentally wrong — entities are dynamic bags of fields defined by the schema. Any TS interface will get out of sync when fields are added/renamed/removed.

## Current problem
- `types/kanban.ts` defines `Task { id, title, description, position, tags, ... }`
- Components read `task.title`, `task.description` as typed properties
- Update calls hardcode field names: `field_name: "title"`, `field_name: "body"`
- None of this consults the field registry
- Custom user fields are invisible to the UI

## What should happen
- The frontend works with `Entity` (entity_type + id + fields: Record<string, unknown>)
- The frontend fetches `EntitySchema` (entity def + field defs) via `get_entity_schema`
- Components render fields by iterating FieldDef[], not by accessing typed properties
- Field name for updates comes from `fieldDef.name`, not a string literal
- Display/editor type comes from `fieldDef.display`/`fieldDef.editor`
- This is the foundation for Phase 3 (Grid) column generation from field registry

## Scope
- Remove or deprecate Task, Tag, Column typed interfaces
- BoardView, TaskCard, TaskDetailPanel, TagInspector render from Entity + FieldDef[]
- get_board and list_tasks return entities with field bags, not typed objects
- Update calls use fieldDef.name from schema
- The card title is just the field where fieldDef.name matches the entity's "title-like" field

## This blocks
- Phase 3 Grid (column generation from field registry)
- Custom user fields
- Field rename/add/delete in the UI
- Correct undo/redo (field names must come from schema)
input.title: Replace typed Task/Tag/Column interfaces with schema-driven Entity rendering

input.description:
The frontend has hardcoded TypeScript interfaces (Task, Tag, Column, Board) that mirror entity fields as typed properties. Entities are dynamic bags of fields defined by the schema — the UI should use `entity_type` as the discriminant and FieldDef[] for rendering, not OO types.

## Design

One type for all entities:
```typescript
interface Entity {
  entity_type: string  // 'task', 'tag', 'column', 'board', etc.
  id: string
  fields: Record<string, unknown>
}
```

The UI knows what KIND of entity it's showing (entity_type = 'task' for board cards, 'column' for lanes, etc.) but accesses field values through the fields bag, not typed properties. Field metadata (name, editor, display, sort) comes from `get_entity_schema(entity_type)`.

## What changes

### Remove
- `interface Task { id, title, description, position, tags, ... }` 
- `interface Tag { id, name, color, ... }`
- `interface Column { id, name, order, ... }`
- All typed property access like `task.title`, `tag.color`, `column.name`

### Keep
- `entity_type` as the string discriminant — the board knows it renders 'task' entities as cards and 'column' entities as lanes
- `FieldDef`, `EntityDef`, `EntitySchema` — the schema types that describe what fields an entity type has
- `Entity` as the single generic type

### Field access pattern
```typescript
// Instead of: task.title
entity.fields[titleFieldDef.name]

// Instead of: field_name: "title" 
field_name: fieldDef.name
```

### Schema loading
- App loads entity schemas on startup via `get_entity_schema`
- Schema context provides field defs to all components
- Components look up field defs by entity_type, not by hardcoded names

## This blocks
- Phase 3 Grid (column generation from field registry)
- Custom user fields
- Field rename/add/delete in the UI
- Correct field-level undo/redo"

input.description:
The native macOS menu (built in Rust via menu.rs) and the command palette (React) are completely separate systems with duplicated functionality. Keymap switching exists in both. Undo/Redo exists in both. Quit only exists in the native menu.

## Design

Commands are the single source of truth. Both the native menu and the command palette derive from the same command registry.

### How it works

1. The command registry lives in React (CommandScope). It already has all the global commands with IDs, names, keybindings.

2. For the native menu: on app startup and when the command set changes, generate the native menu FROM the command registry. This means:
   - Emit commands to the Rust side via a Tauri event or command
   - Rust builds the native menu from the command list
   - Menu item clicks emit back to the frontend as command executions

3. For commands that are OS-level (Quit, Hide, Show All, About): these stay as PredefinedMenuItems but are ALSO registered as commands so they show in the palette.

### Commands to add
- `app.quit` — Quit the application. In palette, executes `window.close()` or Tauri quit API
- `app.keymap.vim` — Switch to vim mode
- `app.keymap.cua` — Switch to CUA mode  
- `app.keymap.emacs` — Switch to emacs mode
- `app.about` — Show about dialog
- `file.new` — New board
- `file.open` — Open board

### Immediate quick fix (before full unification)
- Add app.quit, app.keymap.vim/cua/emacs to the global commands in AppShell
- These just call the existing Tauri commands (set_keymap_mode, etc.)
- The native menu continues to work as-is
- Full menu generation from commands is a future card

## Files
- `ui/src/components/app-shell.tsx` — add missing global commands
- Future: `src/menu.rs` refactor to generate from command list

## Checklist
- [ ] Add app.quit command (calls Tauri quit API)
- [ ] Add app.keymap.vim, app.keymap.cua, app.keymap.emacs commands
- [ ] Add file.new, file.open commands (or placeholders)
- [ ] Add app.about command
- [ ] All show in command palette with correct keybindings
- [ ] Native menu continues to work (no regression)
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Future card: generate native menu from command registry
input.title: Unify native menu and command palette — commands as single source of truth

input.description:
Add backend commands that return raw entity bags via Entity::to_json(). For tasks, inject computed fields (tags, progress, ready, blocked_by, blocks) into the fields map before serialization. No field renaming, no nesting.

New function: enrich_task_entity() in task_helpers.rs — injects computed fields into entity.fields.
New commands: list_entities(entity_type) and get_entity(entity_type, id) in commands.rs.
Register in main.rs.

output.description:
The frontend has hardcoded TypeScript interfaces (Task, Tag, Column, Board) that mirror entity fields as typed properties. Entities are dynamic bags of fields defined by the schema — the UI should use `entity_type` as the discriminant and FieldDef[] for rendering, not OO types.

## Design

One type for all entities:
```typescript
interface Entity {
  entity_type: string  // 'task', 'tag', 'column', 'board', etc.
  id: string
  fields: Record<string, unknown>
}
```

The UI knows what KIND of entity it's showing (entity_type = 'task' for board cards, 'column' for lanes, etc.) but accesses field values through the fields bag, not typed properties. Field metadata (name, editor, display, sort) comes from `get_entity_schema(entity_type)`.

## What changes

### Remove
- `interface Task { id, title, description, position, tags, ... }` 
- `interface Tag { id, name, color, ... }`
- `interface Column { id, name, order, ... }`
- All typed property access like `task.title`, `tag.color`, `column.name`

### Keep
- `entity_type` as the string discriminant — the board knows it renders 'task' entities as cards and 'column' entities as lanes
- `FieldDef`, `EntityDef`, `EntitySchema` — the schema types that describe what fields an entity type has
- `Entity` as the single generic type

### Field access pattern
```typescript
// Instead of: task.title
entity.fields[titleFieldDef.name]

// Instead of: field_name: "title" 
field_name: fieldDef.name
```

### Schema loading
- App loads entity schemas on startup via `get_entity_schema`
- Schema context provides field defs to all components
- Components look up field defs by entity_type, not by hardcoded names

## This blocks
- Phase 3 Grid (column generation from field registry)
- Custom user fields
- Field rename/add/delete in the UI
- Correct field-level undo/redo"
output.title: Replace typed Task/Tag/Column interfaces with schema-driven Entity rendering

input.description:
In `commands.rs`, `list_entities` clones the entire task list (`entities.clone()`) and then calls `enrich_task_entity` for each task. Each enrichment call iterates `all_tasks` for `task_blocks` and `task_blocked_by`, giving O(N^2) complexity. For `get_entity`, it fetches ALL tasks just to enrich a single one. This will be a performance bottleneck for boards with hundreds of tasks.

Consider: (1) pre-building a dependency index `HashMap<String, Vec<String>>` for blocks/blocked_by lookups, or (2) for `get_entity`, only fetching the dependency chain rather than all tasks. #warning
input.title: O(N^2) task enrichment in list_entities and get_entity

input.description: In `App.tsx` line 143, `handleUpdateField` always passes `entity_type: "task"` to `update_entity_field`. If this callback is ever wired to a non-task entity (e.g. tag or column), it will silently update the wrong entity type. The entity type should be derived from the entity being updated, or the callback should accept it as a parameter. Currently TagInspector has its own separate `updateField` that correctly passes `entity_type: "tag"`, so this is not actively buggy — but it is a latent defect waiting to happen as the code evolves. #warning
input.title: handleUpdateField hardcodes entity_type to "task"

input.description:
Throughout the UI components, entity fields are accessed via unchecked type assertions: `entity.fields.title as string`, `entity.fields.tags as string[]`, `entity.fields.order as number`, etc. If the backend sends unexpected data (null, missing field, wrong type), these will silently produce `undefined` or runtime errors.

Consider adding a small set of typed accessor helpers like:
```ts
function getStr(entity: Entity, field: string): string
function getStrList(entity: Entity, field: string): string[]
function getNum(entity: Entity, field: string, fallback: number): number
```

This mirrors the Rust side which already has `entity.get_str()` and `entity.get_string_list()`. Files affected: board-view.tsx, column-view.tsx, task-card.tsx, task-detail-panel.tsx, tag-inspector.tsx, tag-pill.tsx, nav-bar.tsx. #warning
input.title: Pervasive unsafe field access via `as string` / `as string[]` casts

input.description:
Per the Rust review guidelines, bare `.map_err(|e| e.to_string())` loses error context. In `commands.rs`, nearly every command uses this pattern (e.g. lines 54, 123, 144, 175, etc.). When these errors reach the frontend, the user sees raw error messages like "No such file or directory" with no context about which operation failed.

The Tauri command boundary is the application boundary, so `anyhow` with `.context()` would be appropriate here. However, since Tauri commands require `Result<T, String>`, a pragmatic fix is to use `.map_err(|e| format!("failed to {}: {}", description, e))` for key operations. #warning
input.title: Missing error context on Rust .map_err(|e| e.to_string()) chains

output.description:
Per the Rust review guidelines, bare `.map_err(|e| e.to_string())` loses error context. In `commands.rs`, nearly every command uses this pattern (e.g. lines 54, 123, 144, 175, etc.). When these errors reach the frontend, the user sees raw error messages like "No such file or directory" with no context about which operation failed.

The Tauri command boundary is the application boundary, so `anyhow` with `.context()` would be appropriate here. However, since Tauri commands require `Result<T, String>`, a pragmatic fix is to use `.map_err(|e| format!("failed to {}: {}", description, e))` for key operations. #warning

## What changes

### Remove

- `interface Column { id, name, order, ... }`

output.body:
#ui

The frontend has hardcoded TypeScript interfaces (Task, Tag, Column, Board) that mirror entity fields as typed properties. Entities are dynamic bags of fields defined by the schema — the UI should use `entity_type` as the discriminant and FieldDef[] for rendering, not OO types.

## Design

One type for all entities:
```typescript
interface Entity {
  entity_type: string  // 'task', 'tag', 'column', 'board', etc.
  id: string
  fields: Record<string, unknown>
}
```

The UI knows what KIND of entity it's showing (entity_type = 'task' for board cards, 'column' for lanes, etc.) but accesses field values through the fields bag, not typed properties. Field metadata (name, editor, display, sort) comes from `get_entity_schema(entity_type)`.

## What changes

### Remove
- `interface Task { id, title, description, position, tags, ... }` 
- `interface Tag { id, name, color, ... }`
- `interface Column { id, name, order, ... }`
- All typed property access like `task.title`, `tag.color`, `column.name`

### Keep
- `entity_type` as the string discriminant — the board knows it renders 'task' entities as cards and 'column' entities as lanes
- `FieldDef`, `EntityDef`, `EntitySchema` — the schema types that describe what fields an entity type has
- `Entity` as the single generic type

### Field access pattern
```typescript
// Instead of: task.title
entity.fields[titleFieldDef.name]

// Instead of: field_name: "title" 
field_name: fieldDef.name
```

### Schema loading
- App loads entity schemas on startup via `get_entity_schema`
- Schema context provides field defs to all components
- Components look up field defs by entity_type, not by hardcoded names

## This blocks
- Phase 3 Grid (column generation from field registry)
- Custom user fields
- Field rename/add/delete in the UI
- Correct field-level undo/redo"
output.title: Replace typed Task/Tag/Column interfaces with schema-driven Entity rendering.

output.description: In `App.tsx` line 143, `handleUpdateField` always passes `entity_type: "task"` to `update_entity_field`. If this callback is ever wired to a non-task entity (e.g. tag or column), it will silently update the wrong entity type. The entity type should be derived from the entity being updated, or the callback should accept it as a parameter. Currently TagInspector has its own separate `updateField` that correctly passes `entity_type: "tag"`, so this is not actively buggy — but it is a latent defect waiting to happen as the code evolves. #warning
output.title: handleUpdateField hardcodes entity_type to "task"

output.description:
The native macOS menu (built in Rust via menu.rs) and the command palette (React) are completely separate systems with duplicated functionality. Keymap switching exists in both. Undo/Redo exists in both. Quit only exists in the native menu.

## Design

Commands are the single source of truth. Both the native menu and the command palette derive from the same command registry.

### How it works

1. The command registry lives in React (CommandScope). It already has all the global commands with IDs, names, keybindings.

2. For the native menu: on app startup and when the command set changes, generate the native menu FROM the command registry. This means:
   - Emit commands to the Rust side via a Tauri event or command
   - Rust builds the native menu from the command list
   - Menu item clicks emit back to the frontend as command executions

3. For commands that are OS-level (Quit, Hide, Show All, About): these stay as PredefinedMenuItems but are ALSO registered as commands so they show in the palette.

### Commands to add
- `app.quit` — Quit the application. In palette, executes `window.close()` or Tauri quit API
- `app.keymap.vim` — Switch to vim mode
- `app.keymap.cua` — Switch to CUA mode  
- `app.keymap.emacs` — Switch to emacs mode
- `app.about` — Show about dialog
- `file.new` — New board
- `file.open` — Open board

### Immediate quick fix (before full unification)
- Add app.quit, app.keymap.vim/cua/emacs to the global commands in AppShell
- These just call the existing Tauri commands (set_keymap_mode, etc.)
- The native menu continues to work as-is
- Full menu generation from commands is a future card

## Files
- `ui/src/components/app-shell.tsx` — add missing global commands
- Future: `src/menu.rs` refactor to generate from command list

## Checklist
- [ ] Add app.quit command (calls Tauri quit API)
- [ ] Add app.keymap.vim, app.keymap.cua, app.keymap.emacs commands
- [ ] Add file.new, file.open commands (or placeholders)
- [ ] Add app.about command
- [ ] All show in command palette with correct keybindings
- [ ] Native menu continues to work (no regression)
- [ ] Tests
- [ ] Future card: generate native menu from command registry
output.title: Unify native menu and command palette — commands as single source of truth

output.description: All `keybindings.test.ts` test calls fail `tsc --noEmit` because `vi.fn()` is not assignable to `(id: string) => Promise<boolean>`. The mock needs to be typed: `vi.fn<(id: string) => Promise<boolean>>()` or `vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(false) as unknown as ...`. Also, `KeymapMode` is imported but not exported from keybindings.ts and is unused. There is also an unused `vi` import in `undo-stack.test.ts`. #blocker
output.title: Fix TypeScript errors in keybindings.test.ts

output.description:
Throughout the UI components, entity fields are accessed via unchecked type assertions: `entity.fields.title as string`, `entity.fields.tags as string[]`, `entity.fields.order as number`, etc. If the backend sends unexpected data (null, missing field, wrong type), these will silently produce `undefined` or runtime errors.

Consider adding a small set of typed accessor helpers like:
```ts
function getStr(entity: Entity, field: string): string
function getStrList(entity: Entity, field: string): string[]
function getNum(entity: Entity, field: string, fallback: number): number
```

This mirrors the Rust side which already has `entity.get_str()` and `entity.get_string_list()`. Files affected: board-view.tsx, column-view.tsx, task-card.tsx, task-detail-panel.tsx, tag-inspector.tsx, tag-pill.tsx, nav-bar.tsx. #warning
output.title: Pervasive unsafe field access via `as string` / `as string[]` casts

output.description: All `keybindings.test.ts` test calls fail `tsc --noEmit` because `vi.fn()` is not assignable to `(id: string) => Promise<boolean>`. The mock needs to be typed: `vi.fn<(id: string) => Promise<boolean>>()` or `vi.fn().mockResolvedValue(false) as unknown as ...`. Also, `KeymapMode` is imported but not exported from keybindings.ts and is unused. There is also an unused `vi` import in `undo-stack.test.ts`. #blocker
output.title: Fix TypeScript errors in keybindings.test.ts

output.description:
In `commands.rs`, `list_entities` clones the entire task list (`entities.clone()`) and then calls `enrich_task_entity` for each task. Each enrichment call iterates `all_tasks` for `task_blocks` and `task_blocked_by`, giving O(N^2) complexity. For `get_entity`, it fetches ALL tasks just to enrich a single one. This will be a performance bottleneck for boards with hundreds of tasks.

Consider: (1) pre-building a dependency index `HashMap<String, Vec<String>>` for blocks/blocked_by lookups, or (2) for `get_entity`, only fetching the dependency chain rather than all tasks. #warning
output.title: O(N^2) task enrichment in list_entities and get_entity

output.description: In `commands.rs` `get_board_data`, `task_is_ready` is called once per task in the counting loop (line 578), and then again in the summary section (line 640-641). The second pass re-iterates all_tasks for each task. The ready/blocked counts from the first loop could be summed to avoid the second pass. #warning
output.title: get_board_data fetches all tasks twice for ready computation

output.description:
Per the Rust review guidelines, bare `.map_err(|e| e.to_string())` loses error context. In `commands.rs`, nearly every command uses this pattern (e.g. lines 54, 123, 144, 175, etc.). When these errors reach the frontend, the user sees raw error messages like "No such file or directory" with no context about which operation failed.

The Tauri command boundary is the application boundary, so `anyhow` with `.context()` would be appropriate here. However, since Tauri commands require `Result<T, String>`, a pragmatic fix is to use `.map_err(|e| format!("failed to {}: {}", description, e))` for key operations. #warning
output.title: Missing error context on Rust .map_err(|e| e.to_string()) chains

input.description:
Create a single `execute_command` Tauri command that replaces all 14 mutation commands.

```rust
#[tauri::command]
pub async fn execute_command(
    app: AppHandle,
    window: Window,
    state: State<'_, AppState>,
    cmd: String,
    args: Value,
) -> Result<Value, String>
```

Dispatch table (cmd string → existing processor operation):
- `entity.update_field` → UpdateEntityField (replaces update_entity_field)
- `entity.delete` → Delete{Task,Tag,Column,Actor,Swimlane} based on moniker type
- `task.move` → MoveTask (replaces move_task)
- `task.add` → AddTask (replaces add_task)
- `task.untag` → UntagTask (replaces untag_task)
- `tag.update` → UpdateTag (replaces update_tag)
- `column.reorder` → UpdateColumn in a loop (replaces reorder_columns)
- `op.undo` → ectx.undo (replaces undo_operation)
- `op.redo` → ectx.redo (replaces redo_operation)
- `attachment.delete` → DeleteAttachment (replaces delete_attachment)

Args use moniker format where applicable: `{ moniker: "task:01JAB" }` parsed into (entity_type, id).

Also add `show_context_menu` command that takes `items: [{ id, name }]` and shows a native popup, emitting `context-menu-command` with selected id.

- [ ] Add `parse_moniker(s: &str) -> Result<(&str, &str), String>` helper
- [ ] Add `execute_command` with match dispatch
- [ ] Add `show_context_menu` command
- [ ] Register both in main.rs invoke_handler
- [ ] Keep old commands temporarily for backward compat during migration
- [ ] Run tests
input.title: Rust: Add execute_command dispatcher

input.description:
Wrap EntityCard in a CommandScopeProvider with entity-scoped commands.

Create an InspectContext (React context) in App.tsx exposing `inspectEntity(moniker: string)`. This replaces the `onInspect` prop drilling.

EntityCard (or SortableEntityCard) wraps in:
```tsx
<CommandScopeProvider commands={[
  {
    id: "entity.inspect",
    name: `Inspect ${entity.entity_type}`,
    contextMenu: true,
    execute: () => inspectEntity(moniker(entity.entity_type, entity.id)),
  },
]}>
```

Wire `onContextMenu={useContextMenu()}` on the card's root div.

The (i) button can use `useExecuteCommand()` to fire `entity.inspect` instead of calling `onInspect` directly.

- [ ] Provide InspectContext in App.tsx (replace onTaskInspect/onColumnInspect prop drilling)

- [ ] Wire onContextMenu on EntityCard root div
- [ ] Replace (i) button onClick with scope command execution
- [ ] Remove onInspect prop from EntityCard, SortableEntityCard, ColumnView, BoardView
- [ ] Update tests
- [ ] Run tests

After all frontend callers are migrated to execute_command, remove the old individual mutation commands.

Remove from commands.rs:
- `update_entity_field`
- `move_task`
- `add_task`
- `update_tag`
- `untag_task`
- `reorder_columns`
- `delete_task`, `delete_tag`, `delete_column`, `delete_actor`, `delete_swimlane`
- `delete_attachment`
- `undo_operation`, `redo_operation`
- `show_tag_context_menu`

input.description:
Create a single `execute_command` Tauri command that replaces all 14 mutation commands.

```rust
#[tauri::command]
pub async fn execute_command(
    app: AppHandle,
    window: Window,
    state: State<'_, AppState>,
    cmd: String,
    args: Value,
) -> Result<Value, String>
```

Dispatch table (cmd string → existing processor operation):
- `entity.update_field` → UpdateEntityField (replaces update_entity_field)
- `entity.delete` → Delete{Task,Tag,Column,Actor,Swimlane} based on moniker type
- `task.move` → MoveTask (replaces move_task)
- `task.add` → AddTask (replaces add_task)
- `task.untag` → UntagTask (replaces untag_task)
- `tag.update` → UpdateTag (replaces update_tag)
- `column.reorder` → UpdateColumn in a loop (replaces reorder_columns)
- `op.undo` → ectx.undo (replaces undo_operation)
- `op.redo` → ectx.redo (replaces redo_operation)
- `attachment.delete` → DeleteAttachment (replaces delete_attachment)

Args use moniker format where applicable: `{ moniker: "task:01JAB" }` parsed into (entity_type, id).

Also add `show_context_menu` command that takes `items: [{ id, name }]` and shows a native popup, emitting `context-menu-command` with selected id.

- [ ] Add `parse_moniker(s: &str) -> Result<(&str, &str), String>` helper
- [ ] Add `execute_command` with match dispatch
- [ ] Add `show_context_menu` command
- [ ] Register both in main.rs invoke_handler
- [ ] Keep old commands temporarily for backward compat during migration

Tests:
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_valid` — "task:01JAB" → ("task", "01JAB")
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_no_colon` — "badstring" → Err
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_empty_parts` — ":id" and "type:" → Err
- [ ] `test_execute_command_unknown_cmd` — unknown cmd string returns Err("Unknown command: ...")
- [ ] `test_execute_command_entity_delete_routes_by_type` — verify "entity.delete" with moniker "task:X" builds DeleteTask, "tag:X" builds DeleteTag, etc.
- [ ] `test_execute_command_missing_args` — required args missing returns descriptive Err
- [ ] `test_show_context_menu_empty_items` — empty items list is a no-op or returns Ok

input.description:
Create a FocusScope component that manages entity focus (which entity is selected for action) independently from DOM input focus.

**FocusScope component:**
```tsx
interface FocusScopeProps {
  moniker: string;
  commands: CommandDef[];
  children: ReactNode;
  as?: keyof JSX.IntrinsicElements; // default "div"
}
```

FocusScope wraps its children in:
1. `CommandScopeProvider` with the given commands
2. A DOM element that:
   - Sets entity focus on click and contextmenu (not on input focus)
   - Attaches `onContextMenu={useContextMenu()}`
   - Adds `data-focused` attribute when this moniker matches the focused entity
   - Adds `data-moniker` attribute for debugging/CSS

**EntityFocusContext:**
```tsx
interface EntityFocusContextValue {
  focusedMoniker: string | null;
  setFocus: (moniker: string | null) => void;
}
```

Provided once at the App level. Any FocusScope reads from this context and writes to it on click.

**Key behaviors:**
- Click on a FocusScope sets entity focus to its moniker
- Right-click on a FocusScope sets entity focus AND shows context menu
- Clicking an input/textarea inside a FocusScope does NOT change entity focus (only the outermost FocusScope receives the click)
- `useFocusedEntity()` hook returns the current moniker — usable by command palette, vim commands, status bar
- CSS can target `[data-focused]` for visual ring/highlight
- Entity focus is cleared when clicking the backdrop or empty board area

**Interaction with input focus:**
- FocusScope does NOT call `element.focus()` or set tabIndex
- Input focus is managed by individual input components as usual
- Entity focus and input focus are completely independent state

- [ ] Create lib/entity-focus-context.tsx with EntityFocusProvider + useFocusedEntity hook
- [ ] Create components/focus-scope.tsx that combines CommandScopeProvider + entity focus + context menu
- [ ] Provide EntityFocusProvider in App.tsx
- [ ] Clear entity focus on backdrop click

Tests (entity-focus-context.test.tsx):
- [ ] `useFocusedEntity returns null initially` — no entity focused by default
- [ ] `setFocus updates focusedMoniker` — set "task:abc", read back "task:abc"
- [ ] `setFocus(null) clears focus` — clear after setting, read null
- [ ] `useFocusedEntity throws outside provider` — verify error

Tests (focus-scope.test.tsx):
- [ ] `click sets entity focus to moniker` — render FocusScope with moniker "task:abc", click, verify focusedMoniker
- [ ] `right-click sets entity focus and calls show_context_menu` — fire contextmenu, verify both focus set and invoke called
- [ ] `clicking input inside does not change entity focus` — render input inside FocusScope, click input, entity focus unchanged
- [ ] `nested FocusScope: inner click sets inner moniker` — card > tag nesting, click tag, verify tag moniker focused
- [ ] `nested FocusScope: inner right-click stops propagation` — right-click tag, card context menu not triggered
- [ ] `data-focused attribute set when focused` — set focus to moniker, verify attribute present
- [ ] `data-focused attribute absent when not focused` — different moniker focused, verify attribute absent
- [ ] `data-moniker attribute always set` — verify attribute has the moniker value
- [ ] `commands are provided to CommandScopeProvider` — verify commands resolve through scope chain
- [ ] `backdrop click clears entity focus` — click outside all FocusScopes, verify null
input.title: Frontend: FocusScope component + entity focus context

output.description:
Create a single `execute_command` Tauri command that replaces all 14 mutation commands.

```rust
#[tauri::command]
pub async fn execute_command(
    app: AppHandle,
    window: Window,
    state: State<'_, AppState>,
    cmd: String,
    args: Value,
) -> Result<Value, String>
```

Dispatch table (cmd string → existing processor operation):
- `entity.update_field` → UpdateEntityField (replaces update_entity_field)
- `entity.delete` → Delete{Task,Tag,Column,Actor,Swimlane} based on moniker type
- `task.move` → MoveTask (replaces move_task)
- `task.add` → AddTask (replaces add_task)
- `task.untag` → UntagTask (replaces untag_task)
- `tag.update` → UpdateTag (replaces update_tag)
- `column.reorder` → UpdateColumn in a loop (replaces reorder_columns)
- `op.undo` → ectx.undo (replaces undo_operation)
- `op.redo` → ectx.redo (replaces redo_operation)
- `attachment.delete` → DeleteAttachment (replaces delete_attachment)

Args use moniker format where applicable: `{ moniker: "task:01JAB" }` parsed into (entity_type, id).

Also add `show_context_menu` command that takes `items: [{ id, name }]` and shows a native popup, emitting `context-menu-command` with selected id.

- [ ] Add `parse_moniker(s: &str) -> Result<(&str, &str), String>` helper
- [ ] Add `execute_command` with match dispatch
- [ ] Add `show_context_menu` command
- [ ] Register both in main.rs invoke_handler
- [ ] Keep old commands temporarily for backward compat during migration

Tests:
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_valid` — "task:01JAB" → ("task", "01JAB")
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_no_colon` — "badstring" → Err
- [ ] `test_parse_moniker_empty_parts` — ":id" and "type:" → Err
- [ ] `test_execute_command_unknown_cmd` — unknown cmd string returns Err("Unknown command: ...")
- [ ] `test_execute_command_entity_delete_routes_by_type` — verify "entity.delete" with moniker "task:X" builds DeleteTask, "tag:X" builds DeleteTag, etc.
- [ ] `test_execute_command_missing_args` — required args missing returns descriptive Err
- [ ] `test_show_context_menu_empty_items` — empty items list is a no-op or returns Ok
output.title: Rust: Add execute_command dispatcher

output.description:
Create a FocusScope component that manages entity focus (which entity is selected for action) independently from DOM input focus.

**FocusScope component:**
```tsx
interface FocusScopeProps {
  moniker: string;
  commands: CommandDef[];
  children: ReactNode;
  as?: keyof JSX.IntrinsicElements; // default "div"
}
```

FocusScope wraps its children in:
1. `CommandScopeProvider` with the given commands
2. A DOM element that:
   - Sets entity focus on click and contextmenu (not on input focus)
   - Attaches `onContextMenu={useContextMenu()}`
   - Adds `data-focused` attribute when this moniker matches the focused entity
   - Adds `data-moniker` attribute for debugging/CSS

**EntityFocusContext:**
```tsx
interface EntityFocusContextValue {
  focusedMoniker: string | null;
  setFocus: (moniker: string | null) => void;
}
```

Provided once at the App level. Any FocusScope reads from this context and writes to it on click.

**Key behaviors:**
- Click on a FocusScope sets entity focus to its moniker
- Right-click on a FocusScope sets entity focus AND shows context menu
- Clicking an input/textarea inside a FocusScope does NOT change entity focus (only the outermost FocusScope receives the click)
- `useFocusedEntity()` hook returns the current moniker — usable by command palette, vim commands, status bar
- CSS can target `[data-focused]` for visual ring/highlight
- Entity focus is cleared when clicking the backdrop or empty board area

**Interaction with input focus:**
- FocusScope does NOT call `element.focus()` or set tabIndex
- Input focus is managed by individual input components as usual
- Entity focus and input focus are completely independent state

- [ ] Create lib/entity-focus-context.tsx with EntityFocusProvider + useFocusedEntity hook
- [ ] Create components/focus-scope.tsx that combines CommandScopeProvider + entity focus + context menu
- [ ] Provide EntityFocusProvider in App.tsx
- [ ] Clear entity focus on backdrop click

Tests (entity-focus-context.test.tsx):
- [ ] `useFocusedEntity returns null initially` — no entity focused by default
- [ ] `setFocus updates focusedMoniker` — set "task:abc", read back "task:abc"
- [ ] `setFocus(null) clears focus` — clear after setting, read null
- [ ] `useFocusedEntity throws outside provider` — verify error

Tests (focus-scope.test.tsx):
- [ ] `click sets entity focus to moniker` — render FocusScope with moniker "task:abc", click, verify focusedMoniker
- [ ] `right-click sets entity focus and calls show_context_menu` — fire contextmenu, verify both focus set and invoke called
- [ ] `clicking input inside does not change entity focus` — render input inside FocusScope, click input, entity focus unchanged
- [ ] `nested FocusScope: inner click sets inner moniker` — card > tag nesting, click tag, verify tag moniker focused
- [ ] `nested FocusScope: inner right-click stops propagation` — right-click tag, card context menu not triggered
- [ ] `data-focused attribute set when focused` — set focus to moniker, verify attribute present
- [ ] `data-focused attribute absent when not focused` — different moniker focused, verify attribute absent
- [ ] `data-moniker attribute always set` — verify attribute has the moniker value
- [ ] `commands are provided to CommandScopeProvider` — verify commands resolve through scope chain
- [ ] `backdrop click clears entity focus` — click outside all FocusScopes, verify null
output.title: Frontend: FocusScope component + entity focus context

output.description:
Wrap EntityCard in a CommandScopeProvider with entity-scoped commands.

Create an InspectContext (React context) in App.tsx exposing `inspectEntity(moniker: string)`. This replaces the `onInspect` prop drilling.

EntityCard (or SortableEntityCard) wraps in:
```tsx
<CommandScopeProvider commands={[
  {
    id: "entity.inspect",
    name: `Inspect ${entity.entity_type}`,
    contextMenu: true,
    execute: () => inspectEntity(moniker(entity.entity_type, entity.id)),
  },
]}>
```

Wire `onContextMenu={useContextMenu()}` on the card's root div.

The (i) button can use `useExecuteCommand()` to fire `entity.inspect` instead of calling `onInspect` directly.

- [ ] Create InspectContext in a new lib/inspect-context.tsx
- [ ] Provide InspectContext in App.tsx (replace onTaskInspect/onColumnInspect prop drilling)
- [ ] Wrap EntityCard in CommandScopeProvider with entity.inspect
- [ ] Wire onContextMenu on EntityCard root div
- [ ] Replace (i) button onClick with scope command execution
- [ ] Remove onInspect prop from EntityCard, SortableEntityCard, ColumnView, BoardView

Tests (inspect-context.test.tsx — new):
- [ ] `useInspect throws outside provider` — verify error message
- [ ] `inspectEntity parses moniker and calls setPanelStack` — mock context, call with "task:abc", verify panel opened

Tests (entity-card.test.tsx — update existing):
- [ ] `right-click calls show_context_menu with entity.inspect` — mock invoke, fire contextmenu event, verify items contain { id: "entity.inspect", name }
- [ ] `(i) button fires entity.inspect command` — render with InspectContext, click (i), verify inspectEntity called with correct moniker
- [ ] `(i) button renders without onInspect prop` — verify button shows when InspectContext is provided (no prop needed)
- [ ] `entity.inspect command has correct moniker` — render card for entity type "task" with id "abc", verify moniker is "task:abc"
- [ ] `no context menu when no contextMenu commands` — render card without scope, right-click doesn't call invoke

Tests (board-view.test.tsx — update existing):
- [ ] `onTaskInspect prop removed` — verify BoardView no longer accepts onTaskInspect
- [ ] `onColumnInspect prop removed` — verify BoardView no longer accepts onColumnInspect
output.title: Frontend: EntityCard CommandScope + context menu

output.description:
Migrate TagPill from hardcoded native context menu to CommandScope pattern.

TagPill wraps in CommandScopeProvider with:
- `entity.inspect` → inspectEntity(moniker("tag", tag.id)) — contextMenu: true
- `entity.remove` → rustCommand: { cmd: "task.untag", args: { id: parentTaskId, tag: slug } } — contextMenu: true

TagPill needs access to the parent task moniker. It already receives `taskId` as a prop — use that.

Wire `onContextMenu={useContextMenu()}` on the pill span.

Remove:
- The hardcoded `invoke("show_tag_context_menu")` call
- The `tag-context-menu` event listener in App.tsx

- [ ] `entity.remove not available when taskId is undefined` — render without taskId, right-click only shows inspect
- [ ] `context menu stops propagation` — right-click tag doesn't bubble to card's context menu
- [ ] `tag without matching entity still shows context menu` — unknown slug, inspect may be unavailable but no crash
- [ ] `old show_tag_context_menu invoke is gone` — grep test: no references to show_tag_context_menu in tag-pill.tsx

- `move_task`
- `add_task`
- `update_tag`
- `untag_task`
- `reorder_columns`

- `undo_operation`, `redo_operation`
- `show_tag_context_menu`

- All the above command registrations

Remove from menu.rs:
- `handle_tag_menu` function
- tag-specific match arms in `handle_menu_event`

- [ ] Remove tag menu handling from menu.rs

input.description:
When right-clicking a TagPill on an EntityCard, the context menu shows "Inspect task" (from the parent EntityCard's FocusScope) instead of "Inspect tag" (from the TagPill's own FocusScope).

Root cause: The TagPill only registers `entity.inspect` when the tag entity is resolved (`tagMoniker` is truthy). If for any reason the tag lookup fails, the command isn't registered and the parent EntityCard's `entity.inspect` leaks through the scope chain.

Additionally, event propagation may be letting the parent FocusScope's context menu handler fire.

Files: `ui/src/components/tag-pill.tsx`, `ui/src/components/focus-scope.tsx`

- [ ] Always register `entity.inspect` in TagPill scope (use `available: false` when tag not resolved) to block parent scope leakage
- [ ] Add `e.stopPropagation()` in FocusScopeInner's `handleContextMenu` before delegating to useContextMenu as belt-and-suspenders
- [ ] Verify scope chain shadowing works correctly with nested FocusScopes
- [ ] Test: right-click tag shows 'Inspect tag', not 'Inspect task'
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions
input.title: Tag right-click shows 'Inspect task' instead of 'Inspect tag'

input.description:
In vim mode, typing `:` no longer opens the command palette, though `Cmd+Shift+P` still works fine.

The binding table has `":" -> "app.command"` and `normalizeKeyEvent` correctly produces `":"` for Shift+;. The global keydown handler at the document level should match this.

Suspected causes:
1. DOM focus may be landing in an editable element (EditableMarkdown title input) after clicking a card via FocusScope, and the input captures `:` before the global handler
2. The editable element may have a keydown handler with stopPropagation
3. The global handler doesn't skip regular inputs (only `.cm-editor`), so there may be a conflict

Files: `ui/src/lib/keybindings.ts`, `ui/src/components/focus-scope.tsx`, `ui/src/components/editable-markdown.tsx`

- [ ] Investigate where DOM focus lands after clicking a card with FocusScope
- [ ] Check if EditableMarkdown or other editable components intercept single-character keydown events
- [ ] Add input/textarea/select skip logic to the global keydown handler for non-modifier bindings in vim mode
- [ ] Test: click card, then press `:` — palette should open
- [ ] Test: while editing a title, `:` should type normally (not open palette)
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions
input.title: Vim ':' keybinding no longer opens command palette

input.description:
When clicking a card (or tag, or column), entity focus should persist independently of DOM input focus. The command palette (`:` or `Cmd+Shift+P`) and context menu should then show commands scoped to the focused entity AND its containing entities along the React component tree.

Example: clicking a task card, then opening the palette should show:
- Inspect task (from the card's FocusScope)
- Inspect column (from the containing column's FocusScope)
- Inspect board (from the board-level FocusScope)

The context menu should show the same nested inspects — you can inspect any containing entity.

This requires:
1. EntityFocusContext already tracks `focusedMoniker` — but the command palette currently reads from the root scope only, not from the focused entity's scope
2. The command palette needs to resolve commands from the focused entity's scope chain, not just the global scope
3. Each structural container (column, board) needs its own FocusScope with an `entity.inspect` command
4. The context menu and palette should show all non-shadowed `entity.inspect` commands from the scope chain — but since they all have the same ID, they currently shadow each other

Key design question: how to expose multiple inspect commands (task, column, board) when they share the same `entity.inspect` ID. Options:
- Use unique IDs like `entity.inspect.task`, `entity.inspect.column`, `entity.inspect.board`
- Use a single `entity.inspect` per scope but collect ALL from the chain (not just the nearest) for display in palette/menu
- Namespace by moniker: `inspect:task:01ABC`, `inspect:column:backlog`

- [ ] Decide on command ID strategy for multiple inspect commands along the scope chain
- [ ] Add FocusScope to ColumnView with `entity.inspect` for the column
- [ ] Add FocusScope at board level with `entity.inspect` for the board
- [ ] Connect entity focus to command palette — palette reads commands from focused entity's scope chain
- [ ] Connect entity focus to global keydown handler — keybindings resolve through focused scope
- [ ] Update context menu to show all inspect commands from scope chain ancestors
- [ ] Test: click card → palette shows inspect for card, column, and board
- [ ] Test: right-click card → context menu shows nested inspects
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions
input.title: Entity focus scopes command palette and context menu to containing entities

input.description:
## Problem

The current CommandScope system shadows commands by flat string ID. `entity.inspect` in a TagPill's FocusScope blocks `entity.inspect` in the parent EntityCard's FocusScope. But these are *different commands* — they inspect different entities. The scope chain should accumulate them, not shadow them.

## Design

Command identity should be parametric — the verb + moniker target defines uniqueness:
- `entity.inspect` + `tag:xyz` ≠ `entity.inspect` + `task:abc`
- Shadowing only occurs when the full identity matches (same verb, same target entity)
- The palette and context menu collect ALL entity commands up the scope chain

A user clicking a task card, then opening the palette, should see:
- Inspect tag (if a tag was last focused inside the card)
- Inspect task
- Inspect column
- Inspect board

## Approach

Option A: Make command IDs include the moniker — `entity.inspect:tag:xyz`. Shadowing by full ID means different targets never shadow. Simple, but IDs become dynamic.

Option B: Add a `target` field to CommandDef (the moniker). Shadowing checks both `id` and `target`. Commands with the same `id` but different `target` accumulate. More structured.

Option C: Introduce a `group` concept — commands in the same group accumulate instead of shadowing. `entity.inspect` commands are in the "inspect" group.

Preference: Option B — it preserves the existing ID system for non-entity commands (app.quit, app.undo) while adding parametric identity for entity-scoped commands.

## Subtasks

- [ ] Add optional `target?: string` (moniker) field to CommandDef
- [ ] Update `resolveCommand` and `useAvailableCommands` — shadow only when both `id` AND `target` match
- [ ] Update context menu collection to include all accumulated commands
- [ ] Update command palette to show accumulated commands with target context in display
- [ ] Each FocusScope's `entity.inspect` command includes `target: moniker`
- [ ] Add FocusScope to ColumnView with inspect command targeting the column moniker
- [ ] Add FocusScope at board level with inspect command targeting the board moniker
- [ ] Connect entity focus to command palette — palette reads from focused entity's scope chain
- [ ] Connect entity focus to global keydown handler
- [ ] Clicking a tag focuses the tag (already happens via FocusScope)
- [ ] Test: click card → palette shows inspect for tag (if focused), card, column, board
- [ ] Test: right-click → context menu shows all inspects along the React ancestor axis
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions
input.title: Parametric command identity: scope chain accumulates entity commands

output.description:
When right-clicking a TagPill on an EntityCard, the context menu shows "Inspect task" (from the parent EntityCard's FocusScope) instead of "Inspect tag" (from the TagPill's own FocusScope).

Root cause: The TagPill only registers `entity.inspect` when the tag entity is resolved (`tagMoniker` is truthy). If for any reason the tag lookup fails, the command isn't registered and the parent EntityCard's `entity.inspect` leaks through the scope chain.

Additionally, event propagation may be letting the parent FocusScope's context menu handler fire.

Files: `ui/src/components/tag-pill.tsx`, `ui/src/components/focus-scope.tsx`

- [ ] Always register `entity.inspect` in TagPill scope (use `available: false` when tag not resolved) to block parent scope leakage
- [ ] Add `e.stopPropagation()` in FocusScopeInner's `handleContextMenu` before delegating to useContextMenu as belt-and-suspenders
- [ ] Verify scope chain shadowing works correctly with nested FocusScopes
- [ ] Test: right-click tag shows 'Inspect tag', not 'Inspect task'
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions #bug

output.description:
In vim mode, typing `:` no longer opens the command palette, though `Cmd+Shift+P` still works fine.

The binding table has `":" -> "app.command"` and `normalizeKeyEvent` correctly produces `":"` for Shift+;. The global keydown handler at the document level should match this.

Suspected causes:
1. DOM focus may be landing in an editable element (EditableMarkdown title input) after clicking a card via FocusScope, and the input captures `:` before the global handler
2. The editable element may have a keydown handler with stopPropagation
3. The global handler doesn't skip regular inputs (only `.cm-editor`), so there may be a conflict

Files: `ui/src/lib/keybindings.ts`, `ui/src/components/focus-scope.tsx`, `ui/src/components/editable-markdown.tsx`

- [ ] Investigate where DOM focus lands after clicking a card with FocusScope
- [ ] Check if EditableMarkdown or other editable components intercept single-character keydown events
- [ ] Add input/textarea/select skip logic to the global keydown handler for non-modifier bindings in vim mode
- [ ] Test: click card, then press `:` — palette should open
- [ ] Test: while editing a title, `:` should type normally (not open palette)
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions #bug
output.title: Vim ':' keybinding no longer opens command palette

input.description:
## Design

Bridge the gap between entity focus (which FocusScope was clicked) and command resolution (what commands the palette/keybindings can see).

### Registry

`EntityFocusProvider` gains a `Map<string, CommandScope>` ref (not state — no re-renders on register/deregister). Each FocusScope registers `(moniker, scope)` on mount via `useEffect`, deregisters on unmount.

The CommandScope already has a `.parent` pointer built by React's nested CommandScopeProviders, so registering the leaf scope gives access to the full chain.

### Focus resolution

New hook: `useFocusedScope(): CommandScope | null` — looks up `focusedMoniker` in the registry. Returns the focused entity's scope (with its full parent chain) or null if nothing is focused.

The palette and `KeybindingHandler` use `useFocusedScope()` to resolve commands from the focused entity's scope chain, falling back to the root AppShell scope when nothing is focused.

### "Am I focused?" hook

`useIsFocused(moniker): boolean` — returns true if:
- `focusedMoniker === moniker` (direct), OR
- The focused scope's ancestor chain includes this moniker (descendant focus)

This lets a column know "a card inside me is focused" and apply visual styles (e.g., subtle highlight). Cards know if a tag inside them is focused.

The ancestor check walks the registry: look up `focusedMoniker`, then walk `.parent` scopes checking if any FocusScope in the chain has this moniker.

### Unfocus

Click on empty board background → `setFocus(null)`. Add an onClick on the board backdrop area in BoardView.

## Subtasks

- [ ] Add `scopeRegistry: Map<string, CommandScope>` ref to EntityFocusProvider
- [ ] Add `registerScope(moniker, scope)` and `unregisterScope(moniker)` to the context value
- [ ] FocusScope calls registerScope on mount, unregisterScope on unmount (useEffect)
- [ ] Add `useFocusedScope()` hook that looks up focusedMoniker in the registry
- [ ] Add `useIsFocused(moniker)` hook — direct match OR ancestor chain match
- [ ] Wire KeybindingHandler to resolve from focused scope (fallback to root)
- [ ] Wire CommandPalette to show commands from focused scope
- [ ] Add unfocus: click on board background calls setFocus(null)
- [ ] Update data-focused attribute to use useIsFocused (so columns highlight when child is focused)
- [ ] Tests: registry lifecycle, useFocusedScope resolution, useIsFocused ancestor detection, unfocus on background click
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions
input.title: Scope registry: wire entity focus to command resolution

input.description:
## Mental Model

**Focus IS a CommandScope.** Clicking or navigating determines which CommandScope is focused. The moniker is just the address — the scope is the thing.

Everything flows from the focused scope:
- Palette shows commands from focused scope's chain
- Keybindings resolve from focused scope's chain
- Context menu collects from focused scope's chain
- Visual elements ask "is the focused scope me or a descendant of me?"

## Registry

`EntityFocusProvider` gains a `Map<string, CommandScope>` ref (not state — no re-renders on register/deregister). Each FocusScope registers `(moniker, scope)` on mount, deregisters on unmount.

The CommandScope already has a `.parent` pointer built by React's nesting. Registering the leaf gives access to the full chain. Add `moniker?: string` to CommandScope so ancestor walks can identify which scopes are FocusScope-created.

## Hooks

- `useFocusedScope(): CommandScope | null` — looks up focused moniker in registry, returns that scope (with full parent chain) or null
- `useIsFocused(moniker): boolean` — true if focused scope IS this moniker or has this moniker in its ancestor chain

## Wiring

KeyBindingHandler and CommandPalette use `useFocusedScope()` to resolve commands, falling back to root AppShell scope when null.

## Unfocus

Click on empty board background → `setFocus(null)` → focused scope is null → fall back to app scope.

## Subtasks

- [ ] Add `moniker?: string` to CommandScope interface
- [ ] FocusScope passes moniker when creating its CommandScope
- [ ] Add `scopeRegistry: Map<string, CommandScope>` ref to EntityFocusProvider
- [ ] Add `registerScope` / `unregisterScope` to context value
- [ ] FocusScope registers on mount, deregisters on unmount (useEffect)
- [ ] Add `useFocusedScope()` hook
- [ ] Add `useIsFocused(moniker)` hook with ancestor chain walk
- [ ] Wire KeybindingHandler to resolve from focused scope (fallback to root)
- [ ] Wire CommandPalette to show commands from focused scope
- [ ] Add unfocus: click on board background calls setFocus(null)
- [ ] Update data-focused to use useIsFocused (columns highlight when child focused)
- [ ] Tests: registry lifecycle, useFocusedScope, useIsFocused ancestor detection, unfocus
- [ ] Run tests to verify no regressions

- [x] Add `FieldDefId` to swissarmyhammer-fields/src/id_types.rs via define_id!
- [ ] Change FieldDef.id from Ulid to FieldDefId
- [ ] Update FieldsContext id_index and all methods
- [x] Update all test code constructing FieldDef
- [ ] Update kanban defaults.rs
- [ ] Re-export from lib.rs
- [ ] Run full test suite

input.description:
Create a new generic, consumer-agnostic `swissarmyhammer-views` crate following the same pattern as `swissarmyhammer-fields`.

## Key files
- `swissarmyhammer-views/Cargo.toml` — workspace crate, deps: serde, serde_yaml, tracing, ulid
- `swissarmyhammer-views/src/lib.rs` — pub mod types, context, error
- `swissarmyhammer-views/src/types.rs` — ViewDef, ViewId, ViewKind enum
- `swissarmyhammer-views/src/context.rs` — ViewsContext with from_yaml_sources(), name/id indexes
- `swissarmyhammer-views/src/error.rs` — ViewsError, Result type alias

## ViewDef type
```rust
struct ViewDef {
    id: ViewId,          // ULID — stable identity
    name: String,        // Human-facing name
    icon: Option<String>, // Lucide icon name
    kind: ViewKind,      // Grid | Board
    entity_type: String, // Which entity type this view shows
    commands: Vec<String>, // Command IDs to register on mount
}
```

## ViewsContext
- `from_yaml_sources(writable_root, &[(&str, &str)])` — parse YAML, build name+id indexes, later entries override earlier (same merge semantics as FieldsContext)
- `get_view_by_name()`, `get_view_by_id()`, `all_views()`
- `load_yaml_dir()` — reuse pattern from swissarmyhammer-fields

## Checklist
- [ ] Create Cargo.toml with workspace deps
- [ ] Add to workspace Cargo.toml members
- [ ] Implement ViewDef, ViewId, ViewKind types with serde
- [ ] Implement ViewsContext with from_yaml_sources()
- [ ] Implement error types
- [ ] Write tests: YAML parsing, override merge, name/id lookup, invalid YAML skipping
- [ ] cargo test -p swissarmyhammer-views passes

#phase2-views
